summary:
Ephemera are the omission-grazers of the Deep Ethereal: incorporeal or near-incorporeal Aethera that feed on gaps, lacunae, forgotten dreams, unrecorded histories, missing witnesses, unrealized meanings, and the quiet negative space left where something should have been remembered, named, written, or perceived.

They are not ghosts, haunts, invisible fey, astral thought-parasites, dream predators, or ordinary ethereal vermin, though they may be mistaken for any of these. An Ephemera is a deep-mist native whose body is made from thin ethereal absence: vapor, pale membrane, lost context, phase-friction, and meaning that has failed to anchor.

The class is best represented by creatures such as the Lacuna Drifter: a nonviolent incorporeal grazer that does not truly hunt, but drifts through the Deep Ethereal feeding on ambient omission. It may enter the Border Ethereal, haunted houses, ruined archives, sleeping chambers, battlefields, courts, tunnels, and forgotten roads, but these are grazing migrations rather than proof of Borderling ancestry.

An Ephemera’s danger is subtle. It does not usually claw, bite, or drain life. Instead, it makes reality harder to hold in place. A witness misremembers the room. A candle gutters. Dust spirals. Ink pales. A name slips from the mind. A latch falls open. A document seems to have always been blank. A creature becomes briefly desynced from the world and cannot quite react in time.
tax:
Ephemera belong beneath Deep Ethereals because their primary ecology is the far Ethereal: mist-depths, protomatter thin-zones, colorless gaps, abandoned demiplanar margins, and places where events failed to become fully recorded by reality. They may migrate into the Border Ethereal to graze along old buildings, ruins, archives, bedrooms, tomb-edges, or places where memory has thinned, but this is secondary behavior.

The placement test is omission-feeding. If the creature’s body and diet are organized around absence, forgotten context, missing witness, erased record, unanchored perception, or unclaimed meaning, it likely belongs in Ephemera. If it is mainly a wall-native ethereal animal, it belongs among Borderlings. If it is born from dream-symbol and sleep terrain, it belongs among Somnia. If it feeds on thought-routes, projection trails, or silver transit, it belongs among Astralids. If it is the spirit of a dead person, it is not Ephemera by default.

Ephemera are a class rather than a species because the omission-grazing body-plan can produce many forms: lacuna drifters, pale witness-moths, memory-silt rays, blank-mask grazers, margin eels, unscribed hounds, silence-banners, missing-room polyps, and history-thinning shoals. They share a common mode of feeding and manifestation even when their shape, intelligence, and habitat differ.

They should not be treated as fey merely because they are strange, subtle, or nonviolent. A rules chassis may use Fey, Aberration, Outsider, or an Aetheran type depending on system needs, but taxologically they are Deep Ethereal natives.
ap:
Ephemera are usually pale, translucent, and difficult to perceive as complete shapes. Their outlines seem sketched from mist, static, erased ink, frost on glass, or the afterimage left when one looks away from a candle. Many look as if reality has failed to render them for everyone at once.

A typical Ephemera may resemble a drifting humanoid veil, an eyeless animal outline, a jellyfish of blank parchment, a moth made of dust and moonlight, a crouched hound with no facial features, a pale banner moving without wind, or a ribbon-bodied thing full of empty windows. Lacuna Drifters are often medium-sized, incorporeal, and quiet, with a body that appears only to one chosen witness at a time.

Their anatomy is lacunar rather than skeletal. They may have holes where organs should be, faces that become blank when more than one observer looks at them, limbs that fade into margins, or skin patterned with unwritten script. Some contain floating fragments of forgotten dreams, misplaced names, faded heraldry, old lullabies, or diagrams of rooms that no longer exist.

Field signs are subtle and often dismissed. Ink pales. Chalk skips. Dust moves in spirals. Candles gutter without wind. A latch lifts or settles. Curtains shift toward an unseen center. A forgotten object appears slightly displaced. A witness insists something was present, while companions saw only a distortion. Written records lose one adjective, one date, one witness, or one crucial line.

Ephemera are most visible to the creature currently entangled in their perception. To others, they may appear only as environmental effects: disturbed dust, cold mist, pale residue, ripples in glass, a briefly translucent wall, or a hollow in the air where attention refuses to settle.
eco:
Ephemera graze in the Deep Ethereal wherever omission accumulates. Their favored habitats include far-mist margins, unrecorded demiplanar edges, failed archives, abandoned roads, forgotten battle-sites, half-formed rooms, dream-silt deposits, empty courts, erased shrines, dead languages, and ethereal regions where events occurred without witness.

They feed on lacunae: forgotten dreams, missing testimony, unwritten histories, negative space in maps, unremembered rooms, words omitted from records, names no one speaks, stories interrupted before conclusion, and meanings that almost formed but did not. This diet makes them scavengers of reality’s margins rather than predators in the ordinary sense.

A Lacuna Drifter does not normally attack because it does not need violence to feed. It drifts, observes, selects a witness, and grazes on the unstable boundary between what the witness perceives and what others fail to confirm. This grazing may be harmless in small amounts, but repeated exposure can make a creature feel derealized, desynced, or unmoored.

Ephemera reproduce through omission-thickening. A place where records are repeatedly destroyed, witnesses silenced, dreams forgotten, or histories intentionally left blank may shed pale membranes into the Deep Ethereal. These membranes drift, fold, and eventually wake as young Ephemera. Some also bud from older specimens when they consume a sufficiently large gap: a lost treaty, an erased lineage, an unknown grave-field, or a vanished expedition.

Their migrations often follow archives, battlefields, dream-haunted settlements, prisons, sealed classrooms, old roads, abandoned theatres, and places where someone once tried very hard to make an event disappear. They may enter the Border Ethereal during fog, sleep-heavy nights, planar thinning, or after mass forgetting.
beh:
Ephemera are usually quiet, avoidant, and nonconfrontational. They prefer to drift through a scene and graze on omission rather than fight. Their first response to danger is withdrawal, selective manifestation, or perceptual slipperiness.

Most select a single witness at a time. This does not necessarily mean malice. The witness becomes the point of contrast between seen and unseen, remembered and unconfirmed. While the witness perceives the Ephemera, others may see only effects. This isolation is the creature’s grazing posture: it feeds from the gap between private perception and shared reality.

Ephemera rarely understand why material creatures find this distressing. To them, the witness is not being hunted but included. A Lacuna Drifter may hover near a scholar, child, guard, prisoner, or traveler simply because that person is surrounded by unspoken context. It may follow someone who carries a forgotten dream, an unrecorded deed, or a history no one else believes.

When threatened, Ephemera defend themselves by unplacing objects, drinking the gap around an attacker’s certainty, slipping between perceptions, or making reactions fail at the crucial moment. They do not usually open combat. They prefer to make pursuit awkward, memory unreliable, and the environment mildly uncooperative.

Intelligent Ephemera may bargain in omissions. They may ask for a name to be left out of a record, a dream to go unwritten, a witness to be dismissed, a blank space preserved in a map, or the right to graze in an archive without being catalogued. Such bargains are dangerous because what is left unsaid may become food.
traitsText:
**Type Guidance**. Use Aetheran as the preferred taxological type. In systems without that type, Fey, Aberration, Outsider, or an incorporeal ethereal subtype may work mechanically. Do not default to Undead. Ephemera are ghostlike because they are incorporeal and omission-fed, not because they were once alive.

**Deep Ethereal Native**. Ephemera originate in the Deep Ethereal. They may migrate into the Border Ethereal, dreams, ruins, or material-adjacent places, but their primary life cycle belongs to deep mist, far ethereal lacunae, and unanchored omission.

**Incorporeal Body**. Most Ephemera are incorporeal or selectively corporeal. They move through creatures and objects naturally, though ending movement inside solid matter may harm or destabilize them depending on rules system.

**Selective Witnessing**. Many Ephemera are visible to only one chosen witness at a time. Other creatures may notice effects caused by the Ephemera but not its full form. Truesight, see invisibility, ethereal sight, force effects, ghost touch, or specific anchoring rites may bypass this limitation.

**Omission Diet**. Ephemera feed on forgotten dreams, missing records, unspoken histories, blank spaces, unconfirmed perceptions, and meaning that failed to anchor. Lower entries should specify the preferred omission: dream, history, name, map, witness, room, oath, wound, song, or lineage.

**Nonpredatory Default**. Many Ephemera are grazers rather than hunters. They should not be written as aggressive combatants by default. Their danger comes from proximity, perception distortion, and reality-thinning rather than ordinary violence.

**Senses**. Darkvision, ethereal sight, omission-sense, witness-sense, dream-scent, memory-scent, and the ability to detect records, forgotten places, missing names, or unobserved events may be appropriate.

**Defenses**. Incorporeality, invisibility to non-witnesses, resistance to mundane weapons, resistance to elemental damage, immunity to poison, immunity to exhaustion or restraint, and perceptual untargetability may fit many descendants. These defenses should be countered by truesight, see invisibility, force, ghost touch, anchoring magic, restoration of memory, or collective witnessing.

**Weakness Pattern**. Ephemera are vulnerable to anchoring meaning. Recorded testimony, shared witnessing, restored memory, completed maps, named histories, calm emotions, lesser restoration, protection effects, true seeing, force damage, or spells that stabilize mind and reality may suppress their abilities or remove their marks.

**Skills**. Stealth, Perception, Sense Motive, Insight, Knowledge planes, Knowledge history, Knowledge local, Knowledge arcana, Linguistics, and Escape Artist are common directions. Bonuses should favor observation, quiet movement, and detecting what is absent.

**Environment**. Deep Ethereal lacunae, mist margins, colorless gaps, abandoned demiplanar edges, forgotten archives, erased roads, memory-silt fields, ethereal ruins, dream-sediment zones, and Border Ethereal shallows during migration.

**Organization**. Solitary, pair, drift, shoal, witness-line, blank flock, omission herd, lacuna cloud, history-thin procession, or pale migration.
abilities:
**Incorporeal Movement** (Ex or Su). The Ephemera can move through creatures and objects as though they were difficult terrain. If it ends its movement inside a solid object, it suffers force damage, destabilization, or forced ejection according to system needs.

**Selective Manifestation** (Su). At the start of its turn or awareness cycle, the Ephemera chooses one creature it can sense as its Witness. The Ephemera is invisible or indistinct to all others except through secondary effects. Truesight, see invisibility, ethereal sight, or anchoring magic may reveal it.

**Ethereal Grazing** (Ex or Su). The Ephemera feeds passively on ambient omission rather than attacking by default. If destroyed outside its deep-mist anchor, it may dissipate and reform elsewhere in the Deep Ethereal after a short interval unless anchored, named, or dispersed by a restoration effect.

**Aura of Omission** (Su). The Ephemera’s Witness becomes less able to lock reality into stable perception. Failed saves may impose marks or stages such as derealized, desynced, and unmoored. Effects can include penalties to Perception or Investigation, loss of reactions, disadvantage on attacks, or vulnerability to spells until the mind is anchored or rested.

**Lacuna Mark** (Su). Repeated exposure to an Ephemera may place marks of omission on a Witness. These marks should be capped, temporary, and removable by rest, restoration, calm emotions, protection effects, shared witnessing, or successful anchoring. They should inconvenience rather than permanently erase identity unless a stronger descendant explicitly does so.

**Unplace** (Su). The Ephemera can subtly move an unattended object, nudge a latch, tip a cup, slide a book, extinguish or expose a lantern, or cause a small environmental displacement. This should not directly deal damage, but it may create hazards or reveal that the creature is present.

**Drink the Gap** (Su). As a defensive action, the Ephemera feeds on the gap between a Witness’s intention and action. A failed save may prevent reactions, impose disadvantage on the next ability check, disrupt concentration, or briefly make the target unable to complete an immediate response.

**Slip Between** (Su). When damaged, the Ephemera can halve or reduce the harm after other defenses, then become harder to target until its next turn. It may pass through creatures more freely, ignore opportunity attacks, or become intangible to perception. This should be limited to once per round or by recharge.

**Pale Record** (Su). Stronger Ephemera can lighten ink, blur memories, soften inscriptions, or make a witness uncertain whether a detail was ever present. This should be temporary, local, and reversible unless the creature is specifically a history-eater descendant.

**Collective Witness Diminishment** (Su). When multiple creatures accurately perceive, name, and record the Ephemera together, it loses some of its selective manifestation. Effects may include forced visibility, loss of omission aura, suppressed reformation, reduced defenses, or banishment back to the Deep Ethereal.
bg:
Ephemera give the Deep Ethereal a class of quiet incorporeal grazers rather than another family of ghosts or predators. They are built from the Lacuna Drifter model: a creature that feeds from absence, isolates one witness, and disturbs reality through omission rather than violence.

They are useful in play because they create eerie, low-combat encounters. The party may encounter one in a forgotten archive, a room no map includes, a battlefield with missing casualty lists, a dream-silt deposit, a court where one witness was erased, or a ruin where every inscription has one blank line. The threat is not immediate death; it is uncertainty, desynchronization, and the slow thinning of shared reality.

A well-run Ephemera encounter should make players ask what is missing. Why can only one person see it? What record has faded? What event went unwitnessed? What dream was forgotten? What object moved because the creature passed near it? The answer should reveal ecology, not just spooky atmosphere.

They also clarify taxonomy. An incorporeal pale creature that drifts through walls does not have to be undead. A creature that only one person sees does not have to be an illusion. A being associated with forgotten dreams does not have to be Somnia if its life cycle is in the Deep Ethereal and it merely feeds on dream residue.

Ephemera are best used sparingly. Too many can make reality feel arbitrary. A single Lacuna Drifter in a room, a shoal passing through an archive, or a pale migration across a Border Ethereal shallows is usually enough to suggest the vast ecology of omission behind the world.
